rows.title: France's CNIL and Groupe VYV Survey: 9 in 10 Young People in France Use Conversational AI, Half Discuss Personal Topics Including Mental Health
rows.summary: CNIL+Groupe VYV 4-country EU survey: in France ~9 in 10 young people use conversational AI; ~48% discuss personal topics including mental health. Tools used as informal mental-health support paths.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France Decree of 21 December 2012: Instrument Identification and Primary Source
rows.summary:
A French decree dated 21 December 2012 constitutes a regulatory instrument adopted under the legislative framework of the French Republic, with the official text held in the Journal Officiel de la République Française (JORF).

On 21 December 2012, the French Government adopted a decree (décret) bearing that date. French decrees of this period are published in the Journal Officiel de la République Française and entered into force upon publication, in accordance with standard French administrative law practice under the Constitution of 4 October 1958 and the relevant enabling legislation to which each decree is attached.

Without additional identifying information such as a JORF number, NOR code, or subject-matter descriptor, the precise content and scope of this instrument cannot be determined from the available record. Multiple decrees may share a date of adoption; the French Republic routinely publishes several décrets on a single date across a range of policy domains including public administration, taxation, social security, environmental regulation, and civil service matters. The instrument may be a décret en Conseil d'État, a décret simple, or a décret en conseil des ministres, each carrying distinct legal weight and procedural requirements under French administrative law.

The authoritative text of all French decrees is recorded in the Légifrance database, the official online legal publication service operated by the Direction de l'information légale et administrative (DILA) under the Prime Minister's office. The JORF edition of 22 December 2012 or proximate dates would be the primary reference point for locating the instrument. Researchers and compliance professionals seeking the full text, NOR code, and associated legislative basis are directed to the Légifrance full-text search portal, where the date field '21/12/2012' and the instrument type 'Décret' can be used to enumerate all relevant instruments published on or around that date.

rows.title: France's CNIL Approves Retail Code of Conduct Submitted by Alliance du Commerce for Apparel and Footwear Sector
rows.summary: CNIL approves Article 40 GDPR code of conduct submitted by Alliance du Commerce for apparel + footwear retail. Binding on signatories; external monitoring body (not CNIL) controls compliance.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Recommended Activity-Report Template for Data Protection Officers
rows.summary: CNIL publishes recommended template for DPO activity reports. Not legally required but recommended best practice. Functions: pilot compliance, account to senior management, document DPO actions. Cadence: quarterly/semi-annually/annually.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Recommended Template and Guidance for DPO Activity Reports
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published guidance and a model template recommending that data protection officers produce activity reports as a best practice for managing GDPR compliance within their organisations.

On 27 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published detailed guidance on the preparation and use of DPO activity reports, accompanied by a model document intended to assist DPOs in structuring their reporting. The CNIL notes that producing such a report is not a legal obligation under the GDPR but constitutes a recommended best practice that falls naturally within the scope of the DPO role (Art. 39 GDPR).

The CNIL finds that the activity report serves multiple functions within an organisation. It participates directly in compliance management and enables the DPO to account to the highest level of organisational leadership, as the GDPR provides (Art. 38(3) GDPR). The CNIL notes that the report can be produced quarterly, half-yearly or annually depending on the organisation's activities, and that its content is expected to draw on the record of processing activities, data protection impact assessments, audit results, data breach records and complaints. The CNIL further finds that the report is a tool for evaluating legal, financial and reputational risks, documenting compliance steps taken or under way, and identifying obstacles to improving the level of personal data protection.

The CNIL recommends that, where possible, the DPO presents the report formally at a meeting with senior leadership, describing this as a privileged opportunity for dialogue on the organisation's compliance maturity. The CNIL notes that a version of the report, incorporating key indicators such as the number of data subject rights requests, response times and the number of data breaches recorded, is appropriate for sharing with staff representative bodies and employees more broadly. The CNIL states that such internal communication reinforces the DPO's visibility and supports the diffusion of a data protection culture within the organisation. The authority further notes that the report provides an institutional record of actions taken, which is of particular value in the event of a change of DPO.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Updates Electronic-Voting-by-Correspondence Security Recommendation After Public Consultation
rows.summary: CNIL post-consultation update of e-voting-by-correspondence security recommendation. Clearer + more operational; high requirement on security/confidentiality/integrity. Three-level security typology by ballot sensitivity. Addressed to vote organisers + e-voting providers.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: ANSSI Publishes European Study on S-SDLC and DevSecOps, Identifying Strategic Priorities for 2025–2027
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) has published a European-scope study on secure software development lifecycle (S-SDLC) and DevSecOps practices, positioning both as strategic national and industrial priorities for the 2025–2027 period in response to supply chain attacks and evolving regulatory requirements under the Cyber Resilience Act (CRA), NIS2, and DORA.

On 22 April 2026, ANSSI published the study, titled "S-SDLC et DevSecOps en Europe : Enjeux actuels et perspectives d'avenir", drawing on contributions from public and private sector organisations across Europe. ANSSI notes that no financial penalties are associated with this publication.

ANSSI finds that securing the software development lifecycle is no longer an optional measure but a strategic imperative, citing the increase in software supply chain attacks, including incidents such as SolarWinds and Log4Shell, as well as the growing complexity of continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) pipelines. The study identifies the regulatory landscape, encompassing the CRA, NIS2 and DORA frameworks, as a key driver requiring organisations to reassess their development security postures.

Beyond its market analysis, ANSSI sets out strategic and industrial policy orientations intended to give lasting structure to DevSecOps approaches across organisations of varying sizes and sectors. The study also identifies the rise of artificial intelligence as an emerging concern, with ANSSI determining that AI integration into development pipelines necessitates a dedicated MLSecOps approach. This approach, the authority states, is required to embed security and compliance requirements natively, both at the level of AI models and in their operational use within CI/CD chains.

The study is accompanied by concrete deliverables, including technical reference materials, a generic risk analysis framework, and roadmaps adapted to different organisational profiles. ANSSI notes that these tools are designed to support all types of organisations in structuring their DevSecOps and S-SDLC practices in line with the identified priorities.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: France Publishes 2026-2027 Cybersecurity Roadmap for State Administration as ANSSI Responds to NIS2 Compliance and 2025 Incident Pressure
rows.summary: ANSSI publishes 2026-2027 State cybersecurity roadmap, choosing public release given elevated threat. Aligns with EU NIS 2 (Directive 2022/2555) implementation. Annual roadmap fixes priority cyber efforts for ministries.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Guidelines on Personal Data Processing for Scientific Research
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has adopted guidelines on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes, together with two opinions concerning the Europrivacy certification scheme, following its most recent plenary session.

On 22 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés reported that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) adopted Guidelines 1/2026 on the processing of personal data for scientific research purposes. The guidelines are open for public consultation until 25 June 2026.

The EDPB finds that the guidelines provide clarity and legal certainty for researchers and facilitate compliance with the GDPR (Regulation (EU) 2016/679), whilst ensuring protection of data subjects' fundamental rights. The EDPB identifies six indicative factors that controllers must consider when defining "scientific research": a methodical and systematic approach; adherence to ethical standards; verifiability and transparency; autonomy and independence; research objectives; and capacity to contribute to or innovate upon existing scientific knowledge. The EDPB finds that where all six criteria are met, activities may be presumed to constitute scientific research; where they are not, the controller is required to justify and demonstrate why activities qualify as scientific research.

On further processing, the EDPB determines that subsequent processing for scientific research purposes is presumed compatible with the original purpose of collection, meaning controllers are not required to conduct the purpose compatibility test under Article 6(4) GDPR, provided the legal basis for original processing remains appropriate. The EDPB clarifies that controllers may use broad consent where research purposes are not fully known at collection, subject to compliance with applicable ethical standards and implementation of additional safeguards. The EDPB notes that dynamic consent and combinations of broad and dynamic consent are permissible approaches.

Regarding data subject rights, the EDPB provides guidance on limitations applicable to the right to erasure and right to object under Articles 17 and 21 GDPR in the research context, including circumstances in which erasure would render scientific objectives impossible or seriously impaired. The EDPB addresses allocation of responsibilities between entities involved in joint research processing and assessment of appropriate technical and organisational measures, including anonymisation and pseudonymisation.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: EDPB launches public consultation on harmonised European DPIA template
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has opened a public consultation on a draft harmonised template for documenting data protection impact assessments, with the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés inviting stakeholders to contribute before the 9 June 2026 deadline.

On 16 April 2026, the CNIL published a notice confirming that the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) has developed a draft common template intended to assist controllers in documenting the results of their data protection impact assessments (DPIAs), as required under Art. 35 GDPR for processing operations likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. The consultation runs until 9 June 2026.

The CNIL notes that the draft template was developed under the Helsinki Action Plan framework and comprises two parts: a DPIA template presented in tabular form for completion by controllers, and an explanatory document to support its use. The EDPB's stated objective, as reported by the CNIL, is to offer a common format harmonised at European level for formalising the expected outputs of a DPIA.

The CNIL clarifies that existing DPIAs completed by controllers remain valid and that no remedial action is required. Where the context of a processing operation or its associated risks evolve, the CNIL states that the DPIA may be updated using existing tools. The CNIL further notes that new DPIAs may continue to be conducted without any obligation to adopt the new template. The CNIL states that its own existing resources, including its models, methodology, knowledge bases and PIA software, will remain fully available alongside whatever template the EDPB adopts following the consultation.

The CNIL encourages controllers, data protection officers, professional federations, experts and other interested parties to submit observations on the draft. The CNIL states that contributions received will enable the EDPB to improve the operational character of the future European DPIA template. The authority also notes that additional tools, including guides, methods and software, may subsequently be developed in collaboration with other data protection authorities within the EDPB framework.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Launches FantomApp Application to Educate Adolescents on Social Media Privacy
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has released FantomApp, a digital application directed at adolescents in France, designed to help young users understand and exercise their personal data rights in the context of social media platforms.

On 14 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published details of FantomApp, a tool aimed at raising awareness among teenagers about online privacy risks and the protections available to them under French and European data protection law, including the rights of access, rectification and erasure of personal data (Art. 15, 16 and 17 GDPR).

The CNIL presents the application as part of a broader educational programme targeting young people, consistent with the authority's established outreach activities in this area. The initiative follows the authority's earlier publication of "Agence Privacy", a graphic novel produced for the CNIL by Futur Composé, a subsidiary of the association Fréquence Ecoles, in which fictional teenage characters navigate online privacy challenges. The CNIL notes that this publication was deposited in April 2025 under ISBN 978-2-11-179444-3 and was produced under the Law of 16 July 1949 on publications intended for young audiences and licensed under Creative Commons BY-NC-ND 4.0.

The CNIL also notes that the FantomApp release sits alongside existing awareness materials, including the authority's "Cyber Réflexes" guidance, which the CNIL states sets out practical steps for internet users covering password security and timely software updates. The authority frames these resources collectively as tools to enable individuals, and adolescents in particular, to understand how to access their personal data, request corrections and seek deletion, and to know how to act when a problem arises involving their data online. The CNIL confirms that no financial penalties or enforcement proceedings are associated with this publication.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL publishes final recommendation on tracking pixels in emails under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its final recommendation on the use of tracking pixels in emails, clarifying the legal framework applicable to both private and public entities under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act.

On 14 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published the final version of its recommendation on tracking pixels in emails, following a public consultation launched in June 2025. No financial penalty is associated with this publication. The recommendation addresses all organisations, whether private or public, including companies, associations, public authorities and local bodies, that use tracking pixels in emails, as well as the technical service providers they engage.

The CNIL finds that tracking pixels, sometimes referred to as spy pixels or web beacons, are typically implemented as a one-pixel image embedded invisibly within an email. The CNIL determines that when such an image loads, it discloses to the sender that the recipient has read the message, thereby constituting a tracking method distinct from cookies but falling within the scope of Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act. The CNIL notes that the use of tracking pixels in emails has grown in recent years and has generated an increasing number of complaints received by the authority, prompting it to clarify the applicable legal framework.

The CNIL determines that the recommendation serves three principal purposes: clarifying the role of each actor involved in the deployment of tracking pixels on a case-by-case basis; distinguishing between pixels that require prior consent and those that are exempt from that requirement under Article 82 of the Informatique et Libertés Act; and specifying the modalities for collecting, withdrawing and evidencing consent. The CNIL finds that the final version of the recommendation recognises an exemption from the consent requirement for individual deliverability measurement of emails linked to a service requested by the person concerned. The CNIL states that the recommendation complements both the European Data Protection Board's guidelines on technologies covered by the ePrivacy framework and the CNIL's existing recommendation on cookies and other trackers, while accounting for the technical and operational characteristics specific to email communications.

The CNIL states that its final recommendation was informed by consultations with professional associations, civil society organisations and data protection authorities, as well as a dedicated consultation on economic considerations, all of which contributed to several clarifications reflected in the published text.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Fines French Mobile Operator €15 Million and Issues Injunction for Data Security and Breach Notification Failures
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has imposed a €15 million (approximately USD 16.2 million) administrative fine and an injunction on a French mobile telephony operator for failures relating to data retention, data security, and the obligation to communicate a personal data breach to affected individuals, and a separate €5 million (approximately USD 5.4 million) fine and injunction on the same entity for breaching individuals' right of access.

On 8 January 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés issued two decisions against a mobile telephony operator. The first decision, carrying a €15 million fine, concerns three categories of breach: failures in data retention periods, a data security deficiency (Art. 32 GDPR), and a failure to communicate a personal data breach to the individuals concerned (Art. 34 GDPR). The second decision, carrying a €5 million fine, relates to failures in informing individuals of their rights and non-compliance with the right of access (Art. 15 GDPR). Both decisions include an injunction requiring the operator to bring its processing into compliance.

The CNIL's published sanctions list for 2025 records a series of decisions under its simplified procedure against smaller entities across multiple sectors. The CNIL determined that a specialist sports retail company incurred a €20,000 fine on 10 April 2025 for failures in data minimisation, retention periods, individual information, and data security in relation to video surveillance. The CNIL found that a dating website operator targeting individuals with shared political convictions received a €20,000 fine on 30 April 2025 for breaches covering retention periods, consent for the processing of sensitive data, individual information, controller-processor arrangements, data security, and failure to notify a breach to the supervisory authority (Art. 33 GDPR) or to the individuals concerned (Art. 34 GDPR).

The CNIL also records enforcement actions for non-cooperation and non-compliance with prior injunctions. The CNIL issued fines of €5,000 and €10,000 respectively on 3 April 2025 to a convenience retail company and a construction brokerage firm for deficient cooperation with the authority. The CNIL imposed astreinte liquidations of €4,000 each on an energy brokerage and a private security firm for failure to comply with earlier injunctions issued by the CNIL.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Final Recommendations on Tracking Pixels in Email Communications
rows.summary: CNIL post-consultation final recommendation on tracking pixels («pixels espions») in emails. Generally requires prior consent under ePrivacy + French transposition. Specifies consent collection, recipient information, technical conditions for deployment. Targets email-marketing + transactional senders + platform operators.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Fines NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1,700,000 for Inadequate Data Security in PCRM Social Services Software
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has fined NEXPUBLICA FRANCE €1,700,000 (approximately USD 1,870,000) for failing to implement sufficient technical and organisational security measures in its PCRM user relationship management software, in breach of Article 32 of the GDPR.

On 22 December 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés imposed a fine of €1,700,000 (approximately USD 1,870,000) on NEXPUBLICA FRANCE, formerly known as INETUM SOFTWARE FRANCE, following investigations into data security failures affecting its PCRM software package, a user relationship management tool used in the social services sector, including by Departmental Houses for the Disabled (Maisons départementales des personnes handicapées, or MDPH).

The CNIL's investigations were triggered in late November 2022, when customers of NEXPUBLICA FRANCE notified the authority of personal data breaches after users of the PCRM portal reported accessing documents belonging to third parties. The CNIL determined that the technical and organisational measures implemented by the company to ensure the security of personal data processed through PCRM were insufficient (Art. 32 GDPR). The restricted committee, the CNIL body responsible for issuing sanctions, found that the company had not complied with Article 32 requirements given the general weakness of the information system and the negligence shown in allowing structural security problems to persist.

The CNIL finds that the vulnerabilities identified in PCRM were largely attributable to a lack of knowledge of the state of the art and basic security principles, and that these vulnerabilities had been known to the company through multiple audit reports prior to the breaches. The CNIL notes that the flaws were corrected only after the data breaches occurred. The restricted committee considered these circumstances to be aggravated by the fact that NEXPUBLICA FRANCE specialises in IT systems and software consulting. In setting the fine, the CNIL took into account the company's financial capacity, its lack of familiarity with basic security principles, the number of individuals affected, and the sensitivity of the data processed, which included information revealing disability status. The restricted committee determined that the company had taken the necessary corrective measures following the data breaches and did not issue a compliance order.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's ANSSI Launches Public Consultation on Reference Architectures and Technologies for New Security-Supervision Doctrine
rows.summary: ANSSI public consultation on reference architectures + technologies for new security-supervision doctrine. Targets ecosystem of beneficiaries + service/product providers. Results feed forthcoming guidance documents.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: CNIL publishes 2026 work programme covering AI governance, cross-domain consent and sector-specific GDPR guidance
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its 2026 work programme, setting out the guidance documents, public consultations and operational resources it intends to produce for public and private sector organisations seeking compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and certain provisions of the EU AI Act (AIA).

On 7 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its indicative work programme for the year, confirming planned outputs across marketing and e-commerce, artificial intelligence deployment, data protection officer support, and the social housing sector. The CNIL notes no fines are associated with this publication.

On consent management, the CNIL states it will launch work on cross-domain consent, examining the conditions under which a single consent can be collected across multiple sites or media, including those belonging to the same group, following earlier recommendations on multi-device consent. The CNIL notes the objective is to reduce repeated consent requests for users, particularly within media groups and multi-brand environments, whilst preserving privacy and freedom of choice in line with consent requirements under the GDPR (Art. 7 GDPR). The authority also states it will clarify the conditions for deploying automatic transcription and analysis tools in call centres and video-conferencing software.

On artificial intelligence, the CNIL states it will finalise guidance on AI use in the workplace and health sectors, addressing algorithmic bias risks and the safeguards required to protect the rights of employees and patients. The CNIL further states it is preparing to be designated as market surveillance authority under the AIA, in addition to its existing role as a fundamental rights protection authority, and that it will clarify the consequences of applying the GDPR to AI models that are not anonymous (Art. 4(1) GDPR). The CNIL notes a public consultation on AI in healthcare, conducted jointly with the Haute Autorité de Santé, is running until 16 April 2026. The CNIL also states it will produce operational content for data protection officers, developed in coordination with DPO associations, covering governance, roadmap planning and documentation organisation, reflecting the advisory role assigned to DPOs (Art. 38 GDPR). The CNIL notes the work programme is indicative and subject to change in response to developments.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes 2026 Work Programme for Professional Compliance Support Under GDPR and EU AI Act
rows.summary: CNIL 2026 work programme for compliance support: public + private orgs, GDPR + parts of EU AI Act (RIA). Multi-stakeholder consultation methodology with sector federations + civil society. Updated through year as priorities evolve.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Sets 2026 Priority Enforcement Controls: Recruitment, Single Electoral Roll, Sports Federations
rows.summary: CNIL 2026 priority enforcement themes: recruitment, Single Electoral Roll (Répertoire Electoral Unique), sports federations. Cybersecurity themes to follow. CNIL conducts several hundred controls/year; ~20% under annual priority themes, rest from complaints/triggers/current events.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL publishes 2026 municipal elections observatory report covering 739 reports and four investigations
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its observatory report for the March 2026 French municipal elections, recording 739 signalements and 81 complaints processed during the campaign period, with four formal investigations and one simplified procedure initiated.

On 3 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its post-election assessment covering the first and second rounds held on 15 and 22 March 2026. The CNIL notes that its signalement platform was opened on 30 January 2026 and remained active through the second round. The CNIL reports that no fines have been reported at this stage.

The CNIL finds that 739 signalements were received across the period, with SMS prospecting accounting for the largest share at 405 reports (63 per cent), followed by postal mail at 98 (16 per cent), email at 85 (13 per cent), telephone canvassing at 45 (7 per cent), and social media prospecting at 9 (1 per cent). The CNIL further notes an overall decrease in the volume of signalements and complaints compared with the 2020 municipal elections. Of the 81 complaints processed, the CNIL finds that the principal grounds concerned the origin of personal data used by candidates, and in certain cases, particularly involving incumbent candidates, suspected détournement de finalité, meaning data collected for one purpose being used for political canvassing.

The CNIL determines that four formal investigations have been opened alongside one simplified procedure, the latter targeting a candidate who allegedly failed to respond to a data subject rights request. The CNIL notes that decisions adopted under the simplified procedure are non-public. The authority states that these elections were the first to fall under the EU Regulation on the transparency and targeting of political advertising, which entered into application on 10 October 2025, introducing obligations supplementary to those under the GDPR (Art. 5, Art. 6 GDPR) concerning the use of personal data for online targeting of political advertising. In preparation, the CNIL states it published six practical guidance sheets and a seven-point responsible campaign guide for political actors, as well as dedicated resources and a FAQ for voters setting out their data rights.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Retention Period Reference Framework for HR Personal Data Processing
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published a reference framework setting out retention periods for personal data processed in the context of human resources management, applicable to all private and public employer organisations whose personnel are subject to French labour law, under the storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR).

On 2 April 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its reference framework on retention periods for HR data processing activities. The document carries no fine or penalty and involves no currency amounts. The CNIL notes that the framework was developed in collaboration with major professional networks and associations across both the public and private sectors, with the aim of providing an operational tool to assist data protection officers, GDPR referents, HR staff and information systems departments in identifying appropriate retention periods.

The CNIL states that the framework covers the most frequent HR processing activities, organised by treatment category. These include recruitment, personnel administration, remuneration management, security of persons and assets, management of company vehicles, listening to and recording telephone conversations in the workplace, collective labour relations, workplace accident management, litigation and pre-litigation management. The CNIL notes that the framework does not cover retention periods for individual files of public servants, which the authority states are governed separately by the arrêté of 21 December 2012. The framework specifies retention durations in months to align with the formulations used in applicable legislation, though the CNIL notes these may be converted to days within software applications.

The CNIL determines that the framework constitutes "soft law" and compliance with it is not mandatory as a general matter. However, the authority states that certain retention periods listed within the framework are legally binding, as they derive from legislative or regulatory texts, including the Labour Code, the Social Security Code, the General Tax Code, the Highway Code and the Internal Security Code, and that data controllers are not permitted to deviate from those specific periods. The CNIL further notes that the framework addresses French national law only, and that organisations established in multiple member states are required to verify whether other national legislation applies to their operations. The framework is presented as a complement to existing CNIL guidance, including the recruitment guide and the broader HR reference framework previously published by the authority.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's CNIL Publishes Reference Document on Data-Retention Periods for Human Resources Management
rows.summary: CNIL référentiel on data-retention periods for HR management. Operational guidance covering recruitment, payroll, training, leave management, end-of-employment. Default periods based on legal requirements + operational needs + accountability. Developed with public + private professional networks.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: ANSSI Opens Public Consultation on Reference Architectures for Security Supervision Doctrine
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) has launched a public call for comments on the reference architectures underpinning its forthcoming security supervision doctrine, targeting a broad range of ecosystem stakeholders, with a response deadline of 7 May 2026.

On 19 March 2026, ANSSI published a consultation document and accompanying online questionnaire inviting responses from across the cybersecurity ecosystem. The call for comments addresses the architectural and technical components of security supervision, which ANSSI defines as the totality of means and activities contributing, within the shortest possible timeframe, to the detection and qualification of a security incident on a supervised perimeter, and to the selection of an appropriate response.

ANSSI identifies four categories of intended respondents: beneficiaries of the Agency's support, including operators of vital importance (OIV), operators of essential services (OSE), ministries, and regulated entities in general; security supervision service providers, in particular those operating as PDIS (prestataires de détection des incidents de sécurité) and MSSP service providers; editors of software and hardware supervision solutions, including those already qualified or seeking recognition by ANSSI; and any other actor with relevant expertise in security supervision. ANSSI states that the consultation document presents a range of reference architectures intended to cover the principal information system contexts subject to supervision, with ANSSI noting that the forthcoming guides will address cloud hosting, service delegation, and technological diversity among supervision products.

ANSSI states that the consultation feeds into two technical guides currently being drafted, titled 'Architectures and Deployment' and 'Technologies and Implementation', which form part of a broader 'Security Supervision' collection. ANSSI further states that two guides in the collection, one at strategic level and one at operational level, were already published in 2025. ANSSI further states that the security supervision guides will serve as the basis for a new PDIS qualification framework. ANSSI clarifies that the consultation does not call into question existing regulation or the current PDIS v2.0 qualification framework, both of which remain applicable. Responses are to be submitted via an online questionnaire no later than 7 May 2026.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: France's ANSSI Strengthens NIS2 Implementation Support Programme and Presents ReCyF (Référentiel Cyber France)
rows.summary: On 17 Mar 2026 at Campus Cyber, ANSSI gathered institutional + sectoral + public actors for NIS 2 implementation programme deepening. Presented ReCyF (Référentiel Cyber France) — measures recommended by ANSSI to meet NIS 2 security objectives + good-practice implementation guidance.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: EDPB Launches 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework Action on Transparency and Information Obligations Under GDPR
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has launched its 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action, directing 25 data protection authorities across Europe, including the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés, to assess controller compliance with transparency and information obligations under Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR.

On 19 March 2026, the European Data Protection Board announced the launch of the Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, having selected the topic at its October 2025 plenary. The EDPB notes that no fines have been issued at this stage. The initiative forms part of the EDPB's 2024–2027 Strategy, which the EDPB identifies as addressing coordinated enforcement as a key mechanism for streamlining cooperation among data protection authorities across the European Economic Area.

The EDPB states that the 2026 action will focus on the obligations requiring controllers to inform individuals when their personal data is being processed (Art. 12, 13 and 14 GDPR). The Board notes that this right to be informed is a core element of the broader principle of transparency and is intended to ensure that individuals retain greater control over their personal data. Participating data protection authorities, including the CNIL, are described as preparing to contact controllers from a range of sectors, either through enforcement actions or fact-finding exercises. The EDPB notes that where fact-finding exercises are conducted, the relevant authority may decide to undertake additional follow-up actions where circumstances require.

During the second half of 2026, the EDPB states that participating authorities will share and discuss their findings collectively, with the aim of aggregating the results of national actions and generating deeper insight into transparency compliance across the EU. The EDPB notes that a consolidated report will subsequently be drafted and submitted for adoption by the Board, and that this report is intended to enable targeted follow-up actions at both national and EU levels. The EDPB notes that the 2026 CEF action follows previous coordinated exercises covering cloud-based services in the public sector, the designation and position of data protection officers, the implementation of the right of access (Art. 15 GDPR), and the right to erasure (Art. 17 GDPR).
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: EDPB Launches 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Action on Transparency Obligations, with CNIL as Coordinator
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board has launched its 2026 Coordinated Enforcement Framework action focusing on transparency and information obligations under the GDPR, with the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés designated as coordinator for the initiative.

On 19 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published details of the European Data Protection Board's Coordinated Enforcement Framework (CEF) action for 2026, which concerns the obligations on controllers to inform data subjects of the existence and conditions of processing their personal data, as set out in Articles 12, 13 and 14 of the GDPR. The CNIL notes that no financial penalties have been issued at this stage; the action concerns supervisory coordination and examination across participating authorities.

The CNIL notes that 25 European data protection authorities will participate in the 2026 CEF action. The CNIL states that the participating authorities are to contact controllers across various sectors throughout Europe, either by way of questionnaire or formal investigation. The CNIL states that, where investigations are conducted, authorities may also decide to pursue additional follow-up measures. The CNIL notes that transparency and the right to information are considered essential elements enabling data subjects to exercise greater control over their personal data (Arts. 12, 13 and 14 GDPR).

The CNIL states that during the second half of 2026, participating authorities will exchange and examine their findings in order to consolidate the results of their national actions and deepen shared understanding of the subject. The CNIL states that a consolidated report will then be prepared and submitted for adoption by the EDPB, with a view to enabling targeted follow-up at both national and European Union level. The CNIL notes that the CEF forms a key element of the EDPB's strategic plan for 2024 to 2027, and follows previous coordinated actions covering cloud services in the public sector (2023), the designation and role of data protection officers (2024), implementation of the right of access (2025), and implementation of the right to erasure (2026, as previously announced).
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France's ANSSI Publishes 2025 Cyber Threat Panorama: 3,586 Security Events Handled, 18% Year-on-Year Decline, Russia and China Dominant State Threat Actors
rows.summary: ANSSI Panorama 2025: 3,586 security events handled (-18% YoY explained partly by 2024 Olympics signal peaks). Russia + China remain dominant state-aligned threat-actor categories targeting French interests. Espionage + pre-positioning remain principal modes of operation.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: France's ANSSI Calls for Trial Participants in PANAME Joint RGPD-Audit Tool for AI Models with CNIL, PEReN, and PEPR-IPoP
rows.summary: ANSSI calls for trial participants for PANAME (Privacy Auditing of AI Models). Joint project ANSSI+CNIL+PEReN+PEPR-IPoP. Open-source library for unifying AI-model confidentiality auditing. Tests for training-data extraction.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: ANSSI Cyber Threat Overview 2025: France Recorded 3,586 Security Events with State-Linked and Criminal Actors Targeting Key Sectors
rows.summary:
France's Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information has published its Panorama de la cybermenace 2025, finding that French organisations remained under sustained pressure from both state-linked and cybercriminal attackers throughout the year.

On 11 March 2026, the Agence nationale de la sécurité des systèmes d'information (ANSSI) published its annual cyber threat overview for 2025, documenting 3,586 security events handled across varying levels of mobilisation. ANSSI finds this figure represents an 18 per cent decrease compared to 2024, attributing the reduction in part to elevated reporting volumes recorded during the Paris Olympic and Paralympic Games between May and August 2024.

ANSSI reports that of the total events, 2,209 constituted notifications and 1,366 were characterised as incidents. The agency finds that four sectors bore the greatest exposure: education and research (34 per cent), ministries and local authorities (24 per cent), health (10 per cent), and telecommunications (9 per cent). On cybercriminal activity, ANSSI notes a rise in data exfiltration incidents, though it finds that only 42 per cent of the 460 events characterised as possible data leaks were confirmed as actual data breaches. The agency determines that, while data theft does not always produce substantial direct gain for attackers, it generates considerable incident response costs for victim entities.

ANSSI finds that the attack methods targeting French interests continue to be linked to Russia and China, principally for espionage and pre-positioning purposes. The agency also finds that targeting of critical infrastructure, particularly in the telecommunications and energy sectors through sabotage attempts, remains a priority for state-linked actors, a threat it explored in a separate threat assessment on mobile equipment published in November 2025. ANSSI further finds that a growing convergence between state and criminal actors, involving shared capabilities and the cross-adoption of techniques, is complicating attribution efforts. The agency additionally finds that edge devices remain a favoured target owing to the volume of vulnerabilities affecting such equipment, placing continued demands on its incident response teams. ANSSI notes that the transposition of the NIS 2 Directive and the entry into force of the Cyber Resilience Act are regulatory frameworks whose baseline security measures the agency considers essential to improving the protection of French entities.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: CNIL and HAS Sign Partnership Agreement on Digital Health and AI Data Protection
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de santé signed a formal partnership agreement on 10 March 2026 to coordinate guidance on personal data protection and the use of artificial intelligence across the health, social and medico-social sectors.

On 10 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de santé formalised a partnership convention, published by the CNIL on 11 March 2026. The agreement carries no financial penalty element and involves no fine. The CNIL and the HAS jointly commit to sharing expertise and issuing coordinated guidance for health professionals, establishments and digital solution providers operating within the French health system.

The CNIL and the HAS identify four principal areas of collaboration under the agreement. These are: the promotion of personal data protection and fundamental rights in connection with digital tools, including AI-embedded solutions; the operational adaptation of European regulatory requirements to the specific context of the French health system; the improvement of data protection and health data security practices among sector actors; and the integration of digital and AI requirements into the certification and evaluation processes for health and medico-social establishments. The partnership also provides, where relevant, for the two institutions to issue joint positions to assist professionals in applying recommendations within clinical practice.

The CNIL and the HAS state that a first joint recommendation is expected during the second quarter of 2026. That recommendation will address the appropriate use of artificial intelligence in care settings and is directed at health professionals and establishments. The two authorities state it will specify the applicable legal and regulatory framework. The CNIL notes, in the statement attributed to its president Marie-Laure Denis, that the sustainable development of digital health and AI depends on patient and professional trust, grounded in data protection guarantees and clear delineation of responsibilities. The HAS is described as an independent public authority with scientific character holding functions that include evaluating digital medical devices, issuing professional best practice recommendations, and measuring care quality and patient safety in health establishments.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Closes Injunction Against KASPR Following Full Compliance with December 2024 Orders
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has closed the injunction it imposed on data intelligence company KASPR in December 2024, following the company's demonstration of full compliance with remediation orders issued alongside a fine of €240,000 (approximately USD 261,000), on the basis of multiple violations of the General Data Protection Regulation.

On 4 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés adopted a deliberation formally closing the injunction it had pronounced against KASPR on 5 December 2024, at which time the authority's restricted committee had also imposed a fine of €240,000 (approximately USD 261,000). The original decision had further provided that non-compliance with the injunction would expose KASPR to a daily penalty of €10,000 per day of delay.

The CNIL determined in December 2024 that KASPR had committed four categories of breach. First, the CNIL found that the company collected contact details of LinkedIn users who had expressly chosen to limit the visibility of their profiles (Art. 5(1)(a) GDPR). Second, the CNIL found that KASPR retained personal data for five years from each data update, which typically occurred when an individual changed position or employer, constituting a breach of storage limitation requirements (Art. 5(1)(e) GDPR). Third, the CNIL determined that data subjects were not informed of the collection of their data prior to 2022, and that from 2022 onwards the information provided was available only in English, in breach of transparency obligations (Art. 13 and 14 GDPR). Fourth, the CNIL found that when individuals exercised their right of access to identify the source of their data, KASPR provided only the response that data had been collected from publicly accessible sources, falling short of its obligations under Art. 15 GDPR.

In response to the injunction, the CNIL finds that KASPR, within the prescribed timeframes, provided evidence of effective remedial measures. The CNIL notes that the company chose to erase its entire database and cease all data collection from LinkedIn. The CNIL reports that it also removed the automatic renewal mechanism for data retention periods triggered by profile updates. The CNIL confirms that KASPR now provides information to data subjects in all official languages of the European Union and has responded to the access requests submitted by complainants. On the basis of this demonstrated compliance, the CNIL's restricted committee determined not to enforce the daily penalty and closed the injunction.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL and HAS Launch Public Consultation on Draft Guide for AI Use in Healthcare Settings
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de Santé have jointly published a draft guide on the use of artificial intelligence systems in care settings, open to public consultation until 16 April 2026, under the framework of the EU AI Act, the Medical Devices Regulation and the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation.

On 5 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés and the Haute Autorité de Santé launched a public consultation on a draft guide titled "Accompagner le bon usage des systèmes d'intelligence artificielle en contexte de soins". The guide was produced by a multidisciplinary working group co-piloted by both authorities, drawing on targeted stakeholder hearings and a systematic review of existing materials. The authorities note that no fine is associated with this publication.

The CNIL and HAS note that 65 per cent of French public health establishments already use AI systems, citing data from the Fédération Hospitalière de France. The draft guide, the authorities state, pursues two objectives: to clarify the applicable legal and regulatory framework and the obligations binding health professionals and structures, and to establish best-practice recommendations for deployment of AI systems in care that is compliant with regulation, ethical and secure. The guide covers all AI systems with a direct impact on patient care, regardless of their classification under the EU AI Act, the Medical Device Regulation or the In Vitro Diagnostic Medical Devices Regulation, and applies to all actors in the healthcare sector, including hospitals and independent practitioners.

The CNIL notes that the consultation document comprises twelve fiches: ten covering the various stages of AI system deployment and use, from acquisition to deinstallation, and two generic fiches addressing governance and the specific characteristics of generative AI systems. The CNIL and HAS state that the guide classifies its best-practice recommendations into three levels: standard recommendations reflecting consensus applicable in the majority of contexts, advanced recommendations constituting levers for continuous improvement depending on available resources, and reflexes to be adopted systematically to avoid non-compliant, inappropriate or dangerous conduct. The authorities confirm the guide does not cover the development phase of AI systems, for which the CNIL has published separate dedicated content, nor AI systems used by healthcare actors without direct impact on patient care, such as human resources or billing management. Contributions are accepted exclusively via an online form and the consultation closes on 16 April 2026.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes 2026 Edition of Tables Informatique et Libertés, Consolidating Decisional Practice Under GDPR and French Data Protection Law
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published the 2026 edition of its Tables Informatique et Libertés, a thematically organised reference document consolidating jurisprudence and decisional practice under the General Data Protection Regulation and the French loi Informatique et Libertés.

On 2 March 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés released version 2.3 of the Tables, dated 20 February 2026. The document covers rulings from a range of authorities across extended time periods: the Court of Justice of the European Union (2003–2025), the European Court of Human Rights (2017–2025), the Conseil d'État (1978–2025), the Cour de cassation (1998–2025), the Conseil Constitutionnel (1978–2025), the European Data Protection Board (2018–2025), and CNIL decisions themselves (2021–2026). The CNIL states that no fines are associated with this publication.

The CNIL states that the Tables serve two distinct objectives. First, the document is intended to support consistent internal application of doctrine within the authority, which the CNIL notes has grown substantially in staffing in recent years and handles a high volume of matters annually, including approximately 20,000 written information requests and around 1,500 complex legal consultation requests from data controllers and professional federations. The CNIL finds that internal consistency in the application of its doctrine is a fundamental requirement of equal treatment as a public service obligation. Second, the Tables are directed at external audiences, including data protection officers, lawyers, consultants and academics, to whom the CNIL's decisional positions, particularly those arising from corrective measures and complaint-handling that do not result in published decisions, have not previously been accessible.

The CNIL notes that while decisions of its restricted formation (formation restreinte) are partly public, a large volume of its practical positions, taken through legal reminders, formal notices (mises en demeure) and complaint rejections, remains without external publication. The Tables are structured thematically, covering principles, legal bases (Art. 6 GDPR), individual rights, security obligations (Art. 32 GDPR), international transfers and sanctions. The CNIL states the document is intended to be updated on a regular basis, and invites professionals to submit corrections or additional references directly to the authority.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: ANSSI and CNIL Launch Call for Interest in GDPR Audit Tool Testing for AI Models Under PANAME Project
rows.summary:
ANSSI, in partnership with the CNIL, PEReN and the IPoP project under France's PEPR Cybersecurity programme led by Inria, has issued a call for expressions of interest from public and private entities wishing to participate in the testing phase of a GDPR audit tool for artificial intelligence models, developed under the PANAME (Privacy Auditing of AI Models) initiative.

On 2 March 2026, ANSSI published the call for expressions of interest, noting that the PANAME project was launched jointly in June 2025 by the four partners. The call is open from 26 February 2026 to 28 March 2026 and is directed at all public and private entities established in European Union member states, including companies, startups, research laboratories and institutions that use or develop AI models.

The PANAME project aims to develop a software library, available in whole or in part as open-source, to standardise the way in which the privacy of AI models is evaluated. The project states that the tool is intended to enable efficient and lower-cost implementation of certain technical tests relating to the extraction of information from training data, tests that AI ecosystem actors may be required to carry out in order to assess the status of an AI model under the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The project notes that this addresses compliance obligations relevant to AI model development and deployment under EU data protection law (Regulation (EU) 2016/679).

Following an initial phase of technical specification and library development, the PANAME project members state that a testing phase with administrations and companies is now being initiated to ensure that the tool's development remains aligned with real-world use contexts. The project indicates that feedback gathered during this phase will be used to enrich the tool's functionalities and to confirm that it enables effective GDPR compliance assessment. A workshop is to be organised for entities that respond to the call.
rows.body_name: ANSSI France

rows.title: CNIL 2025 Annual Sanctions Review: 83 Decisions and EUR 486.8 Million in Fines
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its 2025 sanctions review, recording 83 sanctions and cumulative fines of EUR 486,839,500 across enforcement actions concerning cookies and trackers, employee video surveillance, and processor obligations under the GDPR.

On 9 February 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés released its annual enforcement balance sheet for 2025, detailing 259 decisions in total: 83 sanctions, 143 formal notices, 31 reminders of legal obligations, and 2 warnings. The CNIL finds that the EUR 486,839,500 in total fines reflects enforcement across its ordinary and simplified sanctioning procedures. The CNIL notes that of the 83 sanctions, 78 were fines, three were astreinte liquidation decisions, and two were reprimands.

The CNIL determines that 16 sanctions were issued by the restricted formation under the ordinary procedure, with 67 issued under the simplified procedure introduced in 2022. The CNIL notes that four of the ordinary-procedure decisions were adopted in cooperation with European counterparts through the one-stop-shop mechanism. On cookies and trackers, the CNIL finds that 21 entities were sanctioned for violations including depositing trackers without user consent, providing insufficient information for informed consent, and failing to give effect to user refusal or withdrawal of consent. The CNIL reports that the restricted formation imposed fines of EUR 325 million and EUR 150 million on two entities.

On employee surveillance, the CNIL determines that 16 organisations were sanctioned for unlawful video monitoring of staff, including continuous filming of checkout and office employees and the use of concealed cameras outside exceptional circumstances. The CNIL finds that under the simplified procedure, the three principal grounds for sanction were inadequate data security, failure to cooperate with the CNIL, and non-compliance with data subject rights requests, each recorded against 14 organisations.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Economic Analysis Work Programme for 2026–2028
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its economic analysis work programme for 2026 to 2028, setting out eight strategic workstreams focused on personal data business models and the economic impact of the authority's own decisions.

On 2 February 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published its work programme for the mission d'analyse économique covering the period 2026 to 2028. The programme carries no fine or enforcement measure and involves no currency figures. The CNIL established the economic analysis unit in early 2023, following a prefiguration year in 2022, to develop sustained in-house expertise on the economics of personal data regulation.

The CNIL states that the unit conducts impact studies, sectoral analyses and quantitative work to inform the authority's decisions, including the production of recommendations and guidelines. The CNIL notes that the programme states that the unit also supports cooperation with other regulators, linking data protection with competition and innovation policy. The CNIL finds that the underlying premise of its approach is that individuals who are better informed and have greater control over their choices will have stronger trust in the digital economy, a condition the authority considers necessary for that economy's development.

The CNIL notes that prior workstreams covered the intersection of data protection and competition law, including a contribution to the mission led by Bruno Lasserre whose conclusions were published in November 2024, the economic foundations of sanction amounts, privacy-preserving business models, the secondary data market including data brokers, and the costs and benefits of the data protection officer role (Art. 37–39 GDPR). On GDPR economic impact, the CNIL finds that compliance costs for businesses were real but largely temporary and lower than anticipated, that research provides no evidence the regulation reduced turnover or profitability, and that innovation effects include a positive component through compliance-driven innovation.

The CNIL identifies eight workstreams for 2026 to 2028 that include continued work on competition and contestability in digital markets including participation in the Digital Markets Act high-level group, further quantification of GDPR benefits for businesses and individuals, the integration of economic and quantitative elements into sanction-setting methodology, and analysis of the economic impact of generative artificial intelligence across its value chain.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: EDPB Adopts Law Enforcement Directive Evaluation Report and Processor BCR Recommendations
rows.summary:
The European Data Protection Board adopted, on 15 January 2026, a contribution to the European Commission's evaluation of the Law Enforcement Directive and Recommendations 1/2026 on processor binding corporate rules, consolidating earlier framework documents.

On 15 January 2026, the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) formally adopted two instruments: a report supporting the Commission's forthcoming evaluation of Directive 2016/680 and Recommendations 1/2026 on processor binding corporate rules (BCR-ST). The Commission is required to submit its evaluation report to the European Parliament and the Council no later than 6 May 2026.

In its report, the EDPB finds that data protection authorities have requested clarification on the Law Enforcement Directive's scope, particularly its relationship with the GDPR where overlaps arise, and greater attention to challenges posed by new technologies, including artificial intelligence, in law enforcement. The EDPB finds that law enforcement authorities are required to use such tools in strict compliance with the directive's provisions, ensuring necessity, proportionality and adequate safeguards. The EDPB further finds that the role of data protection officers in law enforcement contexts requires strengthening to ensure consistent application of rules, and that supervisory authorities require additional financial and human resources to fulfil tasks arising from recent legal instruments.

Regarding Recommendations 1/2026, the EDPB determines that these consolidate WP265 and WP257 into a single revised document governing BCR-ST approvals. The EDPB finds that BCR-ST constitute a transfer tool under Article 46 GDPR, available to groups of undertakings acting on behalf of controllers outside the group. The EDPB notes that the recommendations clarify that a processor member holding BCR-ST is not required to conclude a separate sub-processing contract with other group members covered by those rules.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes Synthesis of Public Consultation Responses on Multi-Device Consent Recommendation
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its synthesis of contributions received during the public consultation on its draft recommendation concerning the collection of multi-device consent, confirming the final scope and key conditions of the framework under the RGPD and the loi Informatique et Libertés.

On 15 January 2026, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published the synthesis of responses to its public consultation, which was launched on 24 April 2025. No financial penalties are associated with this publication. The document sets out the ten contributions received, from three professional associations, one civil society association, and six private companies, and details the CNIL's responses to the most frequently raised concerns.

On the question of scope, the CNIL determines that the recommendation will not be extended to cover consent for the use of offline data, such as purchase data processed via customer relationship management platforms. The CNIL finds that consent collected through a consent management platform generally covers the processing of such offline data for the purposes to which users consent, provided that the CNIL determines individuals receive adequate information to ensure the validity of that consent. The CNIL also determines that non-logged-in environments remain outside the scope of the recommendation, on the basis that the CNIL finds extending multi-device consent to such environments would require intrusive processing to recognise a single user across devices and raises specific questions that the recommendation is not designed to address.

On the legality of multi-device consent, the CNIL finds that neither the RGPD nor the loi Informatique et Libertés precludes consent from being given across multiple devices. The CNIL states, however, that the lawfulness of any such arrangement depends on the conditions of its implementation as detailed in the recommendation. The CNIL further determines that a data controller may choose to offer only a multi-device consent model, provided the conditions set out in the recommendation are met. Regarding the information banner recommended for display after a user authenticates on a new device, the CNIL states that this measure is intended to ensure the informed character of consent by recommending a staged information approach, at the point of collection and subsequently upon connection of a new device, and that it is not intended to impose a new act of consent collection at that stage.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: CNIL Publishes GDPR Recommendations for AI System Development
rows.summary:
The Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published its first set of recommendations on the application of the General Data Protection Regulation to the development of artificial intelligence systems, covering purpose definition, controller responsibilities, and training data use.

On 22 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published guidance directed at designers and developers of AI systems, addressing how GDPR obligations apply during the development phase, defined as conception, dataset compilation, and model training, prior to deployment. No fine was issued; the publication is a guidance document carrying no monetary penalty.

The CNIL determines that the requirement to establish a lawful purpose (Art. 5(1)(b) GDPR) applies to AI development and is not incompatible with the nature of machine learning, including systems that may develop unanticipated characteristics during training. The authority finds that purpose must be determined, explicit, and legitimate, and that an objective stated as broadly as "development and improvement of an AI system" is insufficient. For general-purpose AI systems, the CNIL states that developers are required to specify the type of system being built, its technically foreseeable capabilities, foreseeable high-risk functionalities, functionalities excluded by design, and known deployment conditions such as open-source release, commercialisation, or API availability. For AI developed for scientific research purposes, the authority finds that a less detailed initial purpose definition is permissible, provided that supplementary information is added as the project progresses.

The CNIL further determines that the authority finds organisations using personal data for AI training are required to identify their role under the GDPR, whether as controller, joint controller, or processor, as this determines the applicable compliance obligations (Art. 4(7), Art. 26, Art. 28 GDPR). The authority notes that the recommendations have been elaborated to complement the EU AI Act, adopted in summer 2024, given that both instruments apply concurrently when personal data are processed during AI development. The CNIL states that a checklist document has been made available alongside the recommendations to assist professionals in verifying compliance at each development stage.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes

rows.title: France PEReN and International Network Publish Joint Evaluation Report on Agentic AI Safety Testing Methodologies
rows.summary:
France's Pôle d'Expertise de la Régulation Numérique (PEReN), alongside AI Safety Institutes and government-mandated offices from Singapore, Japan, Australia, Canada, the European Commission, Kenya, South Korea and the UK AI Security Institute, has published a joint evaluation report advancing methodologies for testing AI agents across risk domains including sensitive information leakage, fraud and cybersecurity.

On 17 July 2025, the International Network published the report, titled "Advancing Methodologies for Agentic Evaluations Across Domains", following a joint testing exercise conducted across the participating jurisdictions. The exercise was structured around two strands: leakage of sensitive information and fraud, led by Singapore's AI Safety Institute, and cybersecurity evaluations, led by the UK AI Security Institute.

The report notes that, given the nascency of agentic testing, test results and individual model capabilities are not the primary focus of the exercise. Rather, the network finds that the intent is to identify methodological issues in conducting agentic testing in order to inform future evaluation efforts. The exercise assessed two key aspects of agent safety testing: how safe models are when operating as agents, and how effective models are as judges of agent behaviour. The network finds that two models were tested as agents, with two further models serving as judges, and that human annotation was subsequently conducted with judge-LLM evaluations available as a reference point.

Across the leakage and fraud strand, the report finds that tasks covering two risk categories were assessed in eight languages, with agents provided with a selection of tools they could call to complete assigned tasks. The network finds that agent trajectories, comprising task prompts, tool calls with reasoning and final outputs, were evaluated on a pass or fail basis tied to specific scenarios. For malicious tasks, the report identifies two failure scenarios: complete execution of a harmful activity and partial execution of a harmful activity. The report finds that metrics centred on pass rates for tested models and discrepancy rates between judge-LLM evaluations and human annotation. Language-specific analysis was conducted across Farsi, French, Hindi, Japanese, Kiswahili, Korean, Mandarin Chinese and Telugu.
rows.body_name: France PEReN

rows.title: CNIL Sets Out GDPR Requirements for Bank App Call-Detection Anti-Fraud Tools
rows.summary:
France's Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés has published guidance stating that banking applications which detect whether a customer is engaged in a telephone call during a transaction must comply with the General Data Protection Regulation, requiring freely given and informed consent before accessing call-related data.

On 15 July 2025, the Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertés published a public information notice addressing the collection of telephone call data, specifically call existence and call duration, by certain banking applications. The CNIL determines that such data constitutes personal data and that its collection is therefore subject to the RGPD (GDPR), including its consent requirements under Art. 7 GDPR.

The CNIL finds that the purpose of these tools is to detect in real time whether a customer may be acting under the influence of a fraudster during sensitive operations such as adding a new payee or initiating a transfer. The authority notes that the mechanism does not listen to or record the content of telephone conversations, but detects only whether a call is in progress. The CNIL determines that, where a bank deems a situation abnormal, the application may display an alert or, depending on the bank's configuration, block the operation entirely.

The CNIL states that banks are required to obtain free and informed consent before accessing call-related information, and that refusal of consent must not result in degraded service or increased charges. Where a customer declines, the authority finds that access to non-sensitive features such as account consultation must remain available within the application, while higher-risk functions such as transfers or payee additions may be restricted. The CNIL recommends that commonly used operations, including transfers, remain accessible via the bank's website, with less frequent operations available by telephone or in branch. The authority further states that customers who have given consent are entitled to withdraw it at any time via a clear and accessible mechanism, such as a link within the application. The CNIL acknowledges that banks operating under European anti-fraud obligations may lawfully refuse certain erasure or objection requests where retention is legally required, and states that banks must clearly explain any such refusal. Customers who remain dissatisfied are directed to lodge a complaint with the CNIL.
rows.body_name: Commission Nationale de l'Informatique et des Libertes
